meta.name: PHQ-9 Patient Health Questionnaire
meta.description: The PHQ-9 is a brief, reliable self-report screening tool for depressive symptoms, consisting of 9 items corresponding to the 9 DSM-IV diagnostic criteria for major depressive episodes, used to assess the severity of depressive symptoms over the past two weeks.
meta.author: Kroenke, Spitzer & Williams (2001)
meta.reference: Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JBW. The PHQ-9: validity of a brief depression severity measure. J Gen Intern Med. 2001;16(9):606-613.
meta.instruction: Over the last 2 weeks, how often have you been bothered by any of the following problems?

questions.text: Little interest or pleasure in doing things
Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much
Feeling tired or having little energy
Feeling bad about yourself — or that you are a failure or have let yourself or your family down
Trouble concentrating on things, such as reading the newspaper or watching television
Moving or speaking so slowly that other people could have noticed? Or the opposite — being so fidgety or restless that you have been moving around a lot more than usual
Thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself in some way

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your depression score is within the normal range. No significant depressive symptoms detected. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle
Keep up regular exercise and social activities
Occasional mood fluctuations are normal; allow yourself time to relax
Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy

ranges.label: Mild Depression
ranges.description: You may be experiencing some mild depressive symptoms that may slightly affect daily life. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Practice self-care and maintain a regular sleep schedule and exercise routine
Try talking to a trusted friend or family member
Learn some relaxation and stress-reduction techniques
If symptoms persist, consider consulting a professional
Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling

ranges.label: Moderate Depression
ranges.description: Your depressive symptoms are at a moderate level and may affect work, social life, and daily activities. At this level, feelings of low mood may cause persistent fatigue and reduced interest in activities, potentially affecting daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a counselor or psychiatrist soon
Maintain a regular daily routine
Try physical exercise at least 3 times per week
Avoid alcohol and unhealthy coping strategies
Consider psychotherapy (e.g., cognitive behavioral therapy)

ranges.label: Moderately Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your depressive symptoms are quite severe. Daily functioning may be significantly affected. Please seek professional help. Your score suggests notable emotional distress that may be significantly affecting your quality of life. Reaching out to a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to seek medical attention from a psychiatrist
Medication combined with psychotherapy may be needed
Ensure you have a trusted support person nearby
Avoid being alone; stay connected with others
Schedule an appointment with a mental health professional as soon as possible

ranges.label: Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your depressive symptoms are very severe and may seriously affect daily functioning and quality of life. Please seek immediate professional help. Your score suggests notable emotional distress that may be significantly affecting your quality of life. Reaching out to a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Please contact a psychiatrist or visit a hospital immediately
If you have thoughts of self-harm or suicide, please call a crisis hotline immediately (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Do not stay alone; inform family or friends of your situation
Follow professional treatment plans and take medications as prescribed
Schedule an appointment with a mental health professional as soon as possible
